dialogue.utterance: 911, this is Unit 3 responding to a Sick Person call for a 64-year-old male at --Address Redacted-- in the city of [CITY].
Good morning, sir. I'm Marty, and I'm an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He was sitting on a bench shivering and couldn't move when we found him. I think it's just very cold.
Sir, can you tell me your name? What county are we in? What happened today?
Andy Bray. We're in Lee County. I was trying to walk home and got too cold.
Can you tell me your birthday? What is the date today?
September 9th. I think it's the 6th.
Sir, your airway appears patent. Are you having any difficulty speaking or breathing at the moment?
No, not really. I can speak, but I'm very cold.
Respirations are 23 per minute, even. No cyanosis or signs of tracheal deviation observed.
Sir, you are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event. No signs of altered mental status or loss of consciousness. Are you oriented to all of those right now?
It's always like that for me. Probably just part of old age.
Partner, let's take the vitals now. Note the shivering observed by [VEHICLE_IDENTIFIER] on arrival.
Pulse is 97, thready but present at the radial and peripheral sites.
Respirations remain 23, consistent in depth. Work of breathing is unlabored without labored effort seen.
Blood pressure is 107 over 77.
Your airway, breathing, and circulation are all self-maintained. No signs of trauma or difficulty movement from these areas. You don't have chest pain or difficulty breathing, correct?
Correct. No chest or lung pain, just the shivering and back discomfort.
You are alert, oriented to person, place, time, and event with a GCS of 15.
Can you describe the pain you feel? Is it sharp, burning, or any specific sensation?
It's kind of like a sharp, needle sensation all over. My back also hurts and I rate it a 9 out of 10 in severity.
Blood glucose is 138. Slightly elevated, but not preventing any treatment at this stage.
Basic rhythm is currently sinus rhythm, consistent with your historical record.
No signs of bruising, rashes, or abnormal skin turgor. The skin is normal in appearance.
Pupil reactivity and size couldn't be directly assessed due to background noise and positioning. No signs of head trauma or nuchal stiffness observed.
No abnormal heart sounds on initial assessment. Breath sounds are bilateral and equal.
The back pain you're experiencing, is it constant? How long has it been at this level?
Always, off and on, but the cold just brings it back. It's likely from my spinal issues.
SpO2 is 100%. No signs of desaturation reported by earlier responder.
Partner, place the 4-lead ECG for continuous monitoring now. Also, secure an IV site for possible fluids.
IV was successfully placed in the left forearm with 20-gauge cannula. It's secured using aseptic technique.
Let's start with warmed normal saline. Partner, begin the fluids.
Per findings of low body temperature and shivering, we are following the Environmental - Hypothermia protocol. This seems appropriate for the condition presented.
Pulse is now 93, still thready, but this is consistent with the mild hypothermia noted earlier.
Blood pressure is updated to 115 over 61. Still acceptable under the environmental condition.
Respiratory rate remains 23 and stable, with SpO2 at 98%. It's trending down slightly but within expected range.
No visible improvement in mobility yet. Continue to monitor as we proceed.
Let's go over your history. May I ask how you ended up on the bench?
I was walking home and got tired, so I sat on a bench for 3 hours. But it was cold and I got too weak to stand up on my own.
May I ask, what's your overall medical history? Do you have any current conditions that affect you?
I've got Major Depression, Schizoaffective Disorder, and chronic back pain related to multiple sclerosis. Also, I had bronchitis a month ago.
Do you have any allergies we need to be aware of?
I'm allergic to Penicillin. It needs to be avoided unless it's an emergency and a doctor explicitly says otherwise.
What medications are you taking regularly?
Benztropine, Divalproex Sodium, Olanzapine (Zyprexa), Trazodone (Desyrel), Ativan, and some additional medications for sleep outcomes.
Beyond the cold and back pain, do you have any other symptoms like confusion or nausea?
No, I don't. Just the weakness and the pain in the spine and limbs. Nothing else physical going on.
Thanks. Can we record some basics-your name and age for the nurse handoff report?
I am 64 and male. Name is Andy Bray. I can give you more if you need.
Mr. Bray, can we arrange for you to be taken to [COMPANY_NAME] as the destination?
Yes, that's okay with me. I understand it's the best option available.
Partner, assist Mr. Bray to the stretcher gently. Let's position him with semi-Fowlers for the transfer.
Got it. Securing him in semi-Fowlers with extra blankets to avoid heat loss during the ride.
Mr. Bray, the cabin will be kept warm during transport. Are you comfortable now?
Yes, that feels better. I can manage it from here.
Conditions are stable so far. Vitals are stable and we're ready to move.
The ED at RCH will be informed of your cold exposure, back pain, and medical details. Any more concerns before the trip?
No, I'm fine. Everything seems under control.
Safe travels, Mr. Bray. Let us know if anything new happens.
Facility is aware and prepared for Mr. Bray. Estimated arrival in 8 minutes.
Mr. Bray, are you still feeling the cold in any areas of your body?
I'm feeling warmer now. It's improving as the ride continues.
Transport is going well, and vitals are improving. Mr. Bray is stable at the moment.
Mr. Bray, we will insulate you with additional blankets and activate the truck heat for continued warmth.
Thank you, that helps.
Facility is ready and will care for Mr. Bray appropriately upon arrival.
Respiratory rate now measured at 23 with SpO2 at 98%. Blood pressure continues stable at 115 over 61.
Mr. Bray, have you noticed any improvement in your back pain or mobility since being secured?
Somewhat, still weak but definitely less stiff.
Has your pain fluctuated since arrival, and is it still a 9 out of 10?
Still a 9. The heat has helped a bit.
Will you need to provide more details during arrival? We can go over your history once more if needed.
No, the information shared is sufficient. I can confirm all previous answers again if you need.
The care plan will continue under the Environmental - Hypothermia protocol. No changes to interventions planned until ED.
Mr. Bray is warm, stable, and expectations for transport are set.
Mr. Bray, if you feel anything worsening, right away signal us so we can assist you.
Understood. Thank you, I'm confident this will help.
Facility has current vitals and plan of care. No critical changes reported. All set for transfer.
I'll proceed to document everything now. Let us know if there's anything we've missed.
No, all is good. Thank you again.
We've noted all your concerns and will pass them on accurately. Mr. Bray, we'll see you at the hospital soon.
En route, all systems are stable, and patient is alert and oriented to the same extent as upon arrival.
Inter-facility communication has occurred for a smooth handoff. Let's keep the conversation minimal to conserve your energy.
I can manage that. I trust the crew entirely.
That's good to hear. Continue to breathe slowly and stay as relaxed as possible.
Temperature remains low but shows signs of improvement. No additional interventions needed at this stage.
Appreciate that. I don't need anything else right now.
That's correct, we'll keep our attention on vitals and environmental controls.
Mr. Bray, we've passed half the trip. You'll be at RCH within minutes. Any changes since then?
No changes. All's well. I'm grateful for the help.
Good to hear. I'll double-check the IV and monitor to confirm all are functioning.
The IV is secure, fluids stable. No complications reported up to this point.
Keep up the good work. It's excellent working with professionals like you.
We always appreciate that from patients. Our focus has been, and will remain, your safety.
Destination is approached, and ambulance remains on course.
ED Staff is on standby with the linking care plan ready. You're nearly there.
I only hope they recognize the work done so far as well.
They'll see it all right. The documentation is thorough and accurate.
And the care-is right for you. Mr. Bray, a reminder, keep your hands out, just in case you need anything.
Will do, thanks for the reminder.
Just moments. We are almost at the facility.
